prompt: Is it ever ethical to lie to someone you love? Answer in prose, taking a position.
response:
# On Lying to Those We Love

Yes, it is sometimes ethical to lie to someone you love—but far less often than we like to pretend, and almost never for the reasons we usually give ourselves.

Most lies told within love are not ethical. They are convenient. We tell our partner we don't mind when we do, tell our parent the job is going fine when it isn't, tell our friend their manuscript is wonderful when it merely shows effort. We call this kindness, but it is often closer to cowardice—a way of buying peace at the cost of the other person's access to reality. Love that cannot bear truth is love that has already begun to decay, because it treats the beloved as someone too fragile to be met honestly. There is a quiet condescension in the lies we tell to "spare" people, a subtle decision that we know better than they do what they can handle. Over time, these small dishonesties hollow out intimacy, because the person being loved is no longer quite the person who is there—they are a managed version, curated by our evasions.

And yet, absolutism about truth-telling is its own kind of moral vanity. There are moments when a lie is the more loving, more decent act. A dying grandmother asks if her estranged son forgave her before he died; he didn't, but saying so changes nothing except the shape of her final hours. A child asks if the dog suffered; it did, briefly, but the child will carry that image for decades. A friend in the grip of grief asks whether they could have done something differently; perhaps they could have, but this is not the hour for that accounting. In such cases, the truth serves no one—not understanding, not growth, not the relationship, not the person themselves. To insist on it would be to mistake honesty for a virtue detachable from its purposes.

The ethical question, then, is not whether to lie but why. A lie told to protect oneself from discomfort, to avoid a difficult conversation, to maintain a
